text:
Might it be a good idea not to spin it up at eye level? Eye protection too perhaps?
If you have a pedestal drill, easy to rig up pivoted arm buckets, good for degassing resin.
15cm radius @1000rpm = 168G
15cm radius @ 2000rpm = 671G

.....etc.
Calculators on-line if too lazy to work out.

author: @goldbunny1973
text: I wonder why a centrifuge costs £200 when a Food Processor/Blender which is built to last and sends several kilos of materials flying at 200mph thus generating a ton of centrifugal force with dry material separation potential if adapted (ironic) is £3 from the Charity shop and includes a built-in variable speed motor and lockable protective hard plastic case/cover. Even an inexpensive coffee grinder motor and it's casing are adaptable into a Centrifuge with the advantage that it will look as nice in your Kitchen as sited in your Garage/Workshop : )

author: @ArnoldVeeman
text:
I am new to this, but 12000 rpm, really? On the net I find numbers with a zero less than that being the maximum rpm. I know I am a newby but am I overlooking something here?

Great tip btw!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +ArnoldVeeman my lab homogeniser runs at 13,000 rpm mate - it just depends - the motor was expensive i will say that - glad you liked the tip - sorry about the quality this was an early vid

replies.author: @ArnoldVeeman
replies.text:
+Robert Murray-Smith Thanks for your swift reply. Ah, so you have another motor and this was just about the making of the centrifuge attachment? Cool nevertheless.

I am looking for a engine that can produce high rpms which I can vary with a dimmer switch (I want to isolate proteins from lupine pulp and other beans in this why) but until now I've seen only 2k centrifuges, which were already second hand . . . 

any hints?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +ArnoldVeeman I do two things mate - i make stuff that anyone can make for home use - but i also run a lab and for that i buy what i can afford and what will work well - sometimes this means spending money.  A centrifuge is basically a motor, a cage and protection - a dimmer is a poor way to control a motor if you are asking a lot of it - you're better off with a PWM controller and a brushless dc for the kind of thing you are looking at - these can spin up to 70,000 rpm and come in 48v (or bigger) versions that are really quite powerful - the rest - i am afraid - is up to your ingenuity - I would, however, appreciate a heads up if you build one and do a vid

author: @ClownWhisper
text:
thanks for this, im building one myself as im making tons of magnetite among other things.
i have a big bag of those exact sample tubes myself along with a couple cases of the orange cap conical bottom graduated tubes so ill be making varying sized holes.

my eppendorf only holds the small flip top tubes so that was disappointing, but this will do me fine

replies.author: @ClownWhisper
replies.text:
Got a question for you about this magnetite.
I just made up tons of it and unfortunately I am busy spinning it down 4 tubes at a time...

One thing that I am noticing is that it is spinning down but it is only compacting to like a pudding consistency. 

Also it is oxidizing as it sits in the ramekin .  Its actually rusting pretty fast, which surprises me after using the oxidizers in prior steps.

is that normal?

(never worked with the stuff but I need some for a project.)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Clown Whisper it's normal mate - it will compact more if you spin it faster - try capping it with ascorbic acid to prevent rusting

replies.author: @ClownWhisper
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Brilliant! thanks.
I have also noticed that if I just dont touch it it only rusts on the very top thin layer so I could literally just prpocess the whole lot and then store it down one time and I think it will be fine.

just curious. would over the country  vitamin C do the trick or is there too much crap in it?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Clown Whisper whenever you use impure chemicals you get odd results - it will do it but you can buy pure ascorbic acid pretty easily and cheaply on the net

author: @manwar999
text: take a copper wire bend it back and forth it starts to crack at the bend point and its end to end respectively electrical conductivity resistance increases, till the bend point separates, and then air (if you’re in atmosphere medium) takes over and the resistance then becomes air plus end to end copper both sides = total resistance...what you need is an ink that never dries and is E conductive to all its aggregates its incorporated with...ffree electrons, salt ions what have you, must touch...

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I see what you are saying. I have sorted out the yield and size problems by complexing the copper salt with ammonia. I'm waiting for delivery of some oleic acid to act as an anti-oxidant coating to the copper nanoparticles and as a phase transition agent. it might be an idea to try and polymerise these to get the 'hair' you are suggesting. Anyway - i'll update once i have the acid and have done the experiments - thanks for your comments - they are always useful

author: @manwar999
text: you might need a ball and joint type molecular bond of the conductive particles with free electrons and the adhesive ‘’flexible’’ agent bending as the conductive nano particles roll with the bend to an fro and cohesive/adhesive forces will maintain its integrity thereby preserving conductivity…im thinking like human hair though conductive…what type of animals have skin that is ‘’electrical’’

author: @manwar999
text: you should have your own pbs tv show...I’m suspecting that copper once oxidized won’t work properly in bending, remember when you took that spatula to press (the ink you made) down, that temporarily forced the nano copper to ''touch'' one another...although if you bend the paper i think it might break the bonds created by force thus losing conductivity and thereby increasing the resistance...
